mappings.rationaleAI: Government's constitutional frameworks and forms (democracy, monarchy, autocracy) address conceptions of legitimacy and authority (A.4) and theories of constitutionalism (A.5), while its systemic scope covers the national state in political theory (A).

The state exercising sovereign authority over territory and population directly addresses properties of statehood and sovereignty (A.1), the state and the individual (A.2), and theories of state function including social contract and divine right as bases of legitimacy (A.4).

Democracy as government by popular sovereignty directly addresses continuing consent of the governed (A.4.a) and constitutional government (A.5), while electoral processes connect to political action by organized parties (B.2) and the concept of human rights (C.2).
Monarchy as hereditary sovereign rule directly addresses venerable sanction and constitutional succession (A.4.c), and historically divine right as a source of political authority (A.4.d).
Anarchism as a political philosophy advocating abolition of the state and capitalism is directly addressed under the ideology of anarchism (C.3.b.i).

Liberalism's emphasis on consent of the governed addresses popular sovereignty (A.4.a), social contract theory (A.4.b), individual rights and political equality connect to human rights (C.2), and it is explicitly listed as a current political ideology (C.3.b.v).
Human rights as universally recognized moral principles encompassing civil, political, economic, social, and cultural rights directly addresses the concept of human rights (C.2).

Locke's natural rights and government by consent address popular sovereignty (A.4.a) and social contract theory (A.4.b), while his influence on liberal constitutionalism connects to human rights (C.2) and liberalism (C.3.b.v).
Conservatism as a political philosophy emphasizing tradition, hierarchy, and established authority is directly listed as a current political ideology (C.3.b.iii).
Socialism as a political and economic philosophy defined by social ownership of the means of production is directly listed as a current political ideology (C.3.b.viii).
Liberty as lawful, rights-bounded autonomy addresses the state and the individual (A.2), while negative and positive liberty concepts connect to the concept of human rights (C.2).

Ideology as a structured system of beliefs legitimizing or challenging social arrangements directly addresses modern ideologies (C.3) and the importance of ideology to political systems and movements (C.3.a).
Fascism as a far-right totalitarian ideology combining ultranationalism and authoritarianism is directly listed as a current political ideology (C.3.b.iv).
Marx's historical materialism, class struggle theory, and Communist Manifesto program for revolution are foundational to both communism (C.3.b.ii) and Marxism as political ideology (C.3.b.vi).

The Enlightenment's emphasis on natural rights and constitutional government connects to popular sovereignty (A.4.a), social contract theory (A.4.b), origins of constitutional government (A.5), and the concept of human rights (C.2).
Communism as a political and economic ideology advocating collective ownership and a classless society is directly listed as a current political ideology (C.3.b.ii).
Nationalism as an ideology asserting congruence of nation and state, promoting national sovereignty and self-determination, is directly listed as a current political ideology (C.3.b.vii).
Secularism as the principle of separating governance from religious authority directly addresses the problem of church and state (C.4.a).
Machiavelli's empirical analysis of power, statecraft, and realpolitik addresses the national state in political theory (A) and the concept of political power (C.1).
Social and political power as the capacity to influence others through coercion, institutional structure, and cultural hegemony directly addresses the concept of political power (C.1).
Dictatorship as absolute rule sustained through cooptation and repression addresses conceptions of legitimacy and authority (A.4) and the concept of political power exercised without consent (C.1).
Community as a social unit with shared bonds and collective identity addresses political action within small groups, villages, or communities (B.1).

Politics as the processes of collective decision-making and power distribution broadly encompasses the national state in political theory (A), patterns of political action (B), and political concepts, ideologies, and problems (C).
Wollstonecraft's argument for gender equality grounded in Enlightenment rationalism connects to human rights (C.2) and liberal political theory (C.3.b.v).
The nation as collective social organization defined by shared identity addresses the national state as viewed in political theory (A) and connects to nationalism as an ideology (C.3.b.vii).
The EU as a supranational political union addresses the national state in the international community (A.3) and the problem of international cooperation and integration (C.4.c).
Social equality as equal rights, liberties, and status for all individuals connects directly to the concept of human rights (C.2).
Imperialism as extension of power over foreign peoples addresses the national state in the international community (A.3) and the concept of political power through coercion and hegemony (C.1).
Feminism's pursuit of equal political, economic, and social rights for women, premised on critique of patriarchal structures, directly connects to the concept of human rights (C.2).
The French Revolution's Declaration of the Rights of Man connects to popular sovereignty (A.4.a) and human rights (C.2), while its ideological upheaval shaped modern political ideologies (C.3).
Cities as large, dense settlements with complex governance directly address the urban problem and administration of metropolitan areas (C.4.b).
Property rights mediate the relationship between the state and the individual (A.2) and are central to liberalism's commitment to private property (C.3.b.v).

Political science as the empirical study of governance, power, and political behaviour encompasses the national state in political theory (A), patterns of political action (B), and political concepts and ideologies (C).
